<script async src="https://www.googletagmanager.com/gtag/js?id=G-7PRVEBE1EF"></script>
<script>
  window.dataLayer = window.dataLayer || [];
  function gtag(){dataLayer.push(arguments);}
  gtag('js', new Date());

  gtag('config', 'G-7PRVEBE1EF');
</script>

# Ordinal Logistic Regression {#sec-ordinal-logistic}

```{r}
#| include: false

colourize <- function(x, color) {
  if (knitr::is_latex_output()) {
    sprintf("\\textcolor{%s}{%s}", color, x)
  } else if (knitr::is_html_output()) {
    sprintf("<span style='color: %s;'>%s</span>", color,
      x)
  } else x
}
```

::: {#fig-regression-ordinal-logistic-regression}
```{mermaid}
mindmap
  root((Regression 
  Analysis)
    Continuous <br/>Outcome Y
      {{Unbounded <br/>Outcome Y}}
        )Chapter 3: <br/>Ordinary <br/>Least Squares <br/>Regression(
          (Normal <br/>Outcome Y)
      {{Nonnegative <br/>Outcome Y}}
        )Chapter 4: <br/>Gamma Regression(
          (Gamma <br/>Outcome Y)
      {{Bounded <br/>Outcome Y <br/> between 0 and 1}}
        )Chapter 5: Beta <br/>Regression(
          (Beta <br/>Outcome Y)
      {{Nonnegative <br/>Survival <br/>Time Y}}
        )Chapter 6: <br/>Parametric <br/> Survival <br/>Regression(
          (Exponential <br/>Outcome Y)
          (Weibull <br/>Outcome Y)
          (Lognormal <br/>Outcome Y)
        )Chapter 7: <br/>Semiparametric <br/>Survival <br/>Regression(
          (Cox Proportional <br/>Hazards Model)
            (Hazard Function <br/>Outcome Y)
    Discrete <br/>Outcome Y
      {{Binary <br/>Outcome Y}}
        {{Ungrouped <br/>Data}}
          )Chapter 8: <br/>Binary Logistic <br/>Regression(
            (Bernoulli <br/>Outcome Y)
        {{Grouped <br/>Data}}
          )Chapter 9: <br/>Binomial Logistic <br/>Regression(
            (Binomial <br/>Outcome Y)
      {{Count <br/>Outcome Y}}
        {{Equidispersed <br/>Data}}
          )Chapter 10: <br/>Classical Poisson <br/>Regression(
            (Poisson <br/>Outcome Y)
        {{Overdispersed <br/>Data}}
          )Chapter 11: <br/>Negative Binomial <br/>Regression(
            (Negative Binomial <br/>Outcome Y)
        {{Overdispersed or <br/>Underdispersed <br/>Data}}
          )Chapter 13: <br/>Generalized <br/>Poisson <br/>Regression(
            (Generalized <br/>Poisson <br/>Outcome Y)
        {{Zero Inflated <br/>Data}}
          )Chapter 12: <br/>Zero Inflated <br/>Poisson <br/>Regression(
            (Zero Inflated <br/>Poisson <br/>Outcome Y)
      {{Categorical <br/>Outcome Y}}
        {{Nominal <br/>Outcome Y}}
          )Chapter 14: <br/>Multinomial <br/>Logistic <br/>Regression(
            (Multinomial <br/>Outcome Y)
        {{Ordinal <br/>Outcome Y}}
          )Chapter 15: <br/>Ordinal <br/>Logistic <br/>Regression(
            (Logistic <br/>Distributed <br/>Cumulative Outcome <br/>Probability)
```
:::



```{r setup-r, include=FALSE}
# Load required libraries
library(tidyverse, quietly = TRUE, warn.conflicts = FALSE)
library(ggplot2)
```

```{python setup-py, include=FALSE}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from plotnine import *
from statsmodels.formula.api import glm, ols, mnlogit
from statsmodels.genmod.families import Poisson
from matplotlib import pyplot as plt
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, mean_absolute_error, classification_report
import scipy.stats as stats
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.gridspec as gridspec
from matplotlib.ticker import PercentFormatter
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.metrics import confusion_matrix, ConfusionMatrixDisplay, classification_report, log_loss, accuracy_score, recall_score, f1_score
from sklearn.utils import resample
import statsmodels.api as sm
```

::: {.LO}
::::{.LO-header}
Learning Objectives
::::
::::{.LO-container}
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

- Describe why ordinary **linear models** and **multinomial logistic regression** are inappropriate for **ordered categorical outcomes**.

- Write down the ordinal regression likelihood and the cumulative logit link functions, express cumulative probabilities, and recover category probabilities.

- Understand the **proportional odds assumption** and how it constrains the model (common slope parameters across cutpoints).

- Understand how coefficient estimation via maximum likelihood works in this model.

- Interpret ordinal regression coefficients in real scenarios.

- Use **ordinal logistic regression** for prediction by computing cumulative and category-specific probabilities.

- Assess and diagnose the proportional odds assumption by the **Brant–Wald** test.

- Formulate and interpret **non-proportional odds models** when the proportional odds assumption is violated.

- Evaluate model performance and construct confidence intervals.

::::
:::


## Introduction

**Ordinal logistic regression** is a statistical modeling technique used to analyze relationships between an ordered multi-class categorical response variable and a set of explanatory variables. Unlike **multinomial logistic regression**, which treats categories as unordered, ordinal logistic regression take into account the natural ranking of response levels. Ignoring this ordering may lead to loss of valuable information in both **inference** and **prediction**.

In ordinal outcomes, the information lies **not only** in category membership **but also** in the relative ordering between categories. This structure is modeled through cumulative probabilities, which form the foundation of the cumulative logit (proportional odds) model. Instead of modeling the log-odds of each category relative to a baseline (as we do in multinomial regression), ordinal logistic regression models the **log-odds of being at or below a given category threshold**. That's right! We are looking at a cumulative probabbility now.

A key characteristic of the most commonly used ordinal models is the proportional odds model. It asserts that the effect of predictors is assumed to be constant across all cumulative splits of the response. This assumption plays a central role in the modeling framework and interpretation. To start this chpater let us see some of the assumptions in this model.


### Ordinal Logistic Regression Assumptions

The following are the assumptions that we need to be aware of before fitting this model:

- The response or dependent variable should be measured at the ordinal level with more than two ordered categories. This means there is a natural order between the categories in the response variable. For example likert scale: Strongly agree / Agree / Neutral / Disagree / Strongly Disagree

- There should be one or more independent variables that are continuous, ordinal, or nominal (including dichotomous variables).

- Logit linearity assumption: There must be a linear relationship between any continuous independent variables and the logit of the cumulative probabilities.

- Independent observations: The observations should be independent and the dependent variable should consist of mutually exclusive and exhaustive ordered categories.

- Proportional odds assumption: The relationship between each regressor and the log-odds of being at or below any category is constant across all category thresholds (parallel slopes assumption).

- When the proportional odds assumption is violated, a non-proportional odds model may be considered that allows for some regressors to vary across thresholds.


### Use Cases of Ordinal Logistic Regression

Ordinal logistic regression is particularly useful when the outcome has a meaningful ranking and collapsing categories would discard important structure. Some examples of this are:

1. Likert Scale Survey Responses: A classic example is agreement levels in surveys (Strongly disagree/ Disagree / Neutral / Agree / Strongly Agree). For example, you may study how predictors such as age, education, or exposure to misinformation influence the probability of showing extreme political ideas. 

2. Customer Satisfaction Levels: Marketing analysts may use ordinal regression to understand how service quality, wait time, or pricing influence the likelihood of higher satisfaction ratings.

3. Educational Achievement Levels: Educational researchers may study how study time, attendance in lectures and class activities, or prior preparation affects the probability of achieving higher results in exams.

4. Clinical Severity Scales: Ordinal models help quantify how treatments or patient characteristics influence the likelihood of more severe disease states. These status are often divided into categories such as mild, moderate, or severe.

In all these examples, the ordering is essential. Treating these outcomes as nominal would ignore the structured progression between categories, while treating them as numeric would impose unrealistic equal spacing. Ordinal logistic regression provides a principled framework that respects the ranking while maintaining probabilistic modeling rigor.

## Case Study

We will explore a dataset containing some factors that might influence the decision of college juniors to apply to graduate school. This dataset provides a classic example of an ordinal response, such that the categories are ordered in the response variable. We explore two primary research questions based on this dataset.

- How are a student's GPA, GRE score, and field of study **associated** with their likelihood of applying to graduate school?
- Can we accurately **predict** a student's likelihood of applying to graduate school based on their GPA, GRE score, and field of study?

These questions correspond to two distinct **statistical objectives**:
- **Inference**: Estimate the association between explanatory variables (GPA, GRE score, and field of study) and the likelihood of applying to graduate school.
- **Prediction**: Develop a classification model that classifies individuals into their most probable ordinal category for the likelihood of applying to graduate school: `Low`, `Medium`, `High`, or `Very High`.

## Data Collection and Wrangling

The dataset contains the following variables:

- `likelihood_to_apply`: how likely the student will apply to graduate school, **a discrete and ordinal response with four increasing categories** (`Low`, `Medium`, `High` and `Very High`).
- `GPA`: the student's current GPA, **a continuous explanatory variable**.
- `GRE_score`: the student's most recent GRE score, **a continuous explanatory variable**.
- `field_of_study`: the student's undergraduate field of study, **a categorical explanatory variable with four categories** (Arts, Business, Social Sciences, and STEM).

::: {.panel-tabset}

## R Code

```{.r}
college_data <- read_csv("data/college_data.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
cat("There are", nrow(college_data), "observations and", ncol(college_data), "variables in the dataset.")
print(college_data)
```

## Python Code

```{.python}
url = "https://github.com/alexrod61/regression-cookbook/blob/main/book/data/college_data.csv"
college_data = pd.read_csv(url)
print(f"There are {college_data.shape[0]} observations and {college_data.shape[1]} variables in the dataset.")
print(college_data)
```

:::

::: {.panel-tabset}

## R Output

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
college_data <- read_csv("data/college_data.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
cat("There are", nrow(college_data), "observations and", ncol(college_data), "variables in the dataset.")
print(college_data)
```

## Python Output

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
url = "https://github.com/alexrod61/regression-cookbook/blob/main/book/data/college_data.csv"
college_data = pd.read_csv(url)
print(f"There are {college_data.shape[0]} observations and {college_data.shape[1]} variables in the dataset.")
print(college_data)
```

:::

In this example, the variable `likelihood_to_apply` will be our response of interest. Let's set it up as an **ordered factor** via the `as.ordered()` function in R and `pd.Categorical()` with `ordered=True` in Python.

::: {.panel-tabset}

## R Code

```{.r}
# Factorize the response variable and print its levels
college_data$likelihood_to_apply <- as.ordered(college_data$likelihood_to_apply)
college_data$likelihood_to_apply <- fct_relevel(
  college_data$likelihood_to_apply,
  c("Low", "Medium", "High", "Very High")
)
levels(college_data$likelihood_to_apply)
```

## Python Code

```{.python}
# Factorize the response variable and print its levels
ordered_categories = ["Low", "Medium", "High", "Very High"]
college_data["likelihood_to_apply"] = pd.Categorical(
    college_data["likelihood_to_apply"],
    categories=ordered_categories,
    ordered=True
)
print(college_data["likelihood_to_apply"].cat.categories.tolist())
```

:::

::: {.panel-tabset}

## R Output

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
college_data$likelihood_to_apply <- as.ordered(college_data$likelihood_to_apply)
college_data$likelihood_to_apply <- fct_relevel(
  college_data$likelihood_to_apply,
  c("Low", "Medium", "High", "Very High")
)
levels(college_data$likelihood_to_apply)
```

## Python Output

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
ordered_categories = ["Low", "Medium", "High", "Very High"]
college_data["likelihood_to_apply"] = pd.Categorical(
    college_data["likelihood_to_apply"],
    categories=ordered_categories,
    ordered=True
)
print(college_data["likelihood_to_apply"].cat.categories.tolist())
```

:::

## Exploratory Data Analysis

To visualize the relationship between `likelihood_to_apply` and each continuous explanatory variable (`GPA` and `GRE_score`), we will use the following side-by-side plots:

- **Boxplots.**
- **Violin plots.**

To visualize the relationship between `likelihood_to_apply` and `field_of_study`, we will use stacked bar charts. Each bar will show the percentages (depicted on the $y$-axis) of college students falling on each ordered category of `likelihood_to_apply` with the categories found in `field_of_study` on the $x$-axis. This class of bars will allow us to compare both levels of `field_of_study` in terms of the categories of `likelihood_to_apply`. 

::: {.panel-tabset}

## R Code

```{.r}
college_data_side_boxplots <- college_data |>
  ggplot(aes(likelihood_to_apply, GPA)) +
  geom_boxplot(aes(fill = likelihood_to_apply)) +
  labs(y = "GPA", x = "Likelihood to Apply to Graduate School") +
  ggtitle("Side-by-Side Boxplots") +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 19, face = "bold"),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 17),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 18),
    legend.position = "none"
  ) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette="BuPu")

college_data_side_violin <- college_data |>
  ggplot(aes(likelihood_to_apply, GPA)) +
  geom_violin(aes(fill = likelihood_to_apply)) +
  labs(y = "GPA", x = "Likelihood to Apply to Graduate School") +
  ggtitle("Side-by-Side Violin Plots") +
  stat_summary(
    fun = mean, colour = "red", geom = "point",
    shape = 18, size = 5
  ) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 19, face = "bold"),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 17),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 18),
    legend.position = "none"
  ) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette="BuPu")

# Data wrangling
college_data_prop_summary <- as.data.frame(xtabs(
  ~ field_of_study +
    likelihood_to_apply,
  college_data
) / rowSums(xtabs(
  ~ field_of_study + likelihood_to_apply,
  college_data
)), responseName = "prop")

college_data_stacked_bars <- ggplot(college_data_prop_summary, aes(
  x = field_of_study,
  y = prop, fill = likelihood_to_apply
)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", width = 0.7, colour = "black") +
  geom_text(aes(label = ifelse(prop >= 0.05, paste0(sprintf("%.0f", prop * 100), "%"), "")),
    position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5), colour = "firebrick3", fontface = "bold", size = 6
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  labs(y = "Percent", x = "Field of Study", fill = "") +
  ggtitle("Stacked Bar Charts") +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 19, face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 17, angle = 0),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 17, angle = 0),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 18),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 18, margin = margin(r = 1, unit = "cm")),
    legend.title = element_text(size = 17, face = "bold")
  ) +
  guides(fill = guide_legend(title = "Likelihood to Apply")) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Blues")

plot_grid(college_data_side_boxplots, college_data_side_violin, nrow = 1)
college_data_stacked_bars
```

## Python Code

```{.py}
# ── 1. Side-by-Side Boxplots & Violin Plots ──────────────────────────────────

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 6))

# --- Boxplot ---
sns.boxplot(data=college_data, x='likelihood_to_apply', y='GPA', hue='likelihood_to_apply',
            palette='BuPu', ax=axes[0], legend=False)
axes[0].set_title('Side-by-Side Boxplots', fontsize=19, fontweight='bold')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Likelihood to Apply to Graduate School', fontsize=18)
axes[0].set_ylabel('GPA', fontsize=17)

# --- Violin Plot with mean point ---
sns.violinplot(data=college_data, x='likelihood_to_apply', y='GPA', hue='likelihood_to_apply',
               palette='BuPu', ax=axes[1], legend=False)
means = college_data.groupby('likelihood_to_apply')['GPA'].mean()
for i, (cat, mean_val) in enumerate(means.items()):
    axes[1].plot(i, mean_val, 'o', color='red', markersize=5, zorder=5)
axes[1].set_title('Side-by-Side Violin Plots', fontsize=19, fontweight='bold')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Likelihood to Apply to Graduate School', fontsize=18)
axes[1].set_ylabel('GPA', fontsize=17)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# ── 2. Stacked Bar Chart ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────

# Data wrangling — equivalent to xtabs + prop.table
prop_summary = (
    college_data.groupby(['field_of_study', 'likelihood_to_apply'])
    .size()
    .groupby(level=0)
    .apply(lambda x: x / x.sum())
    .reset_index(name='prop')
)

# Pivot for stacked bar
pivot = prop_summary.pivot(index='field_of_study', columns='likelihood_to_apply', values='prop').fillna(0)

# Plot
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
colors = sns.color_palette('Blues', n_colors=len(pivot.columns))
pivot.plot(kind='bar', stacked=True, ax=ax, color=colors, width=0.7,
           edgecolor='black')

# Add percentage labels
for bar_group in ax.containers:
    for bar in bar_group:
        h = bar.get_height()
        if h >= 0.05:
            ax.text(
                bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2,
                bar.get_y() + h / 2,
                f'{h:.0%}',
                ha='center', va='center',
                color='firebrick', fontweight='bold', fontsize=6
            )

ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(PercentFormatter(xmax=1))
ax.set_title('Stacked Bar Charts', fontsize=19, fontweight='bold')
ax.set_xlabel('Field of Study', fontsize=18)
ax.set_ylabel('Percent', fontsize=17)
ax.tick_params(axis='x', labelrotation=0, labelsize=17)
ax.tick_params(axis='y', labelsize=17)
ax.legend(title='Likelihood to Apply', fontsize=18, title_fontsize=17,
          bbox_to_anchor=(1.01, 1), loc='upper left')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

:::

::: {.panel-tabset}

## R Output

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
college_data_side_boxplots <- college_data |>
  ggplot(aes(likelihood_to_apply, GPA)) +
  geom_boxplot(aes(fill = likelihood_to_apply)) +
  labs(y = "GPA", x = "Likelihood to Apply to Graduate School") +
  ggtitle("Side-by-Side Boxplots") +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 19, face = "bold"),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 17),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 18),
    legend.position = "none"
  ) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette="BuPu")

college_data_side_violin <- college_data |>
  ggplot(aes(likelihood_to_apply, GPA)) +
  geom_violin(aes(fill = likelihood_to_apply)) +
  labs(y = "GPA", x = "Likelihood to Apply to Graduate School") +
  ggtitle("Side-by-Side Violin Plots") +
  stat_summary(
    fun = mean, colour = "red", geom = "point",
    shape = 18, size = 5
  ) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 19, face = "bold"),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 17),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 18),
    legend.position = "none"
  ) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette="BuPu")

# Data wrangling
college_data_prop_summary <- as.data.frame(xtabs(
  ~ field_of_study +
    likelihood_to_apply,
  college_data
) / rowSums(xtabs(
  ~ field_of_study + likelihood_to_apply,
  college_data
)), responseName = "prop")

college_data_stacked_bars <- ggplot(college_data_prop_summary, aes(
  x = field_of_study,
  y = prop, fill = likelihood_to_apply
)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", width = 0.7, colour = "black") +
  geom_text(aes(label = ifelse(prop >= 0.05, paste0(sprintf("%.0f", prop * 100), "%"), "")),
    position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5), colour = "firebrick3", fontface = "bold", size = 6
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  labs(y = "Percent", x = "Field of Study", fill = "") +
  ggtitle("Stacked Bar Charts") +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 19, face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 17, angle = 0),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 17, angle = 0),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 18),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 18, margin = margin(r = 1, unit = "cm")),
    legend.title = element_text(size = 17, face = "bold")
  ) +
  guides(fill = guide_legend(title = "Likelihood to Apply")) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Blues")

plot_grid(college_data_side_boxplots, college_data_side_violin, nrow = 1)
college_data_stacked_bars
```

## Python Output

```{py}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
# ── 1. Side-by-Side Boxplots & Violin Plots ──────────────────────────────────

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 6))

# --- Boxplot ---
sns.boxplot(data=college_data, x='likelihood_to_apply', y='GPA', hue='likelihood_to_apply',
            palette='BuPu', ax=axes[0], legend=False)
axes[0].set_title('Side-by-Side Boxplots', fontsize=19, fontweight='bold')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Likelihood to Apply to Graduate School', fontsize=18)
axes[0].set_ylabel('GPA', fontsize=17)

# --- Violin Plot with mean point ---
sns.violinplot(data=college_data, x='likelihood_to_apply', y='GPA', hue='likelihood_to_apply',
               palette='BuPu', ax=axes[1], legend=False)
means = college_data.groupby('likelihood_to_apply')['GPA'].mean()
for i, (cat, mean_val) in enumerate(means.items()):
    axes[1].plot(i, mean_val, 'o', color='red', markersize=5, zorder=5)
axes[1].set_title('Side-by-Side Violin Plots', fontsize=19, fontweight='bold')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Likelihood to Apply to Graduate School', fontsize=18)
axes[1].set_ylabel('GPA', fontsize=17)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# ── 2. Stacked Bar Chart ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────

# Data wrangling — equivalent to xtabs + prop.table
prop_summary = (
    college_data.groupby(['field_of_study', 'likelihood_to_apply'])
    .size()
    .groupby(level=0)
    .apply(lambda x: x / x.sum())
    .reset_index(name='prop')
)

# Pivot for stacked bar
pivot = prop_summary.pivot(index='field_of_study', columns='likelihood_to_apply', values='prop').fillna(0)

# Plot
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
colors = sns.color_palette('Blues', n_colors=len(pivot.columns))
pivot.plot(kind='bar', stacked=True, ax=ax, color=colors, width=0.7,
           edgecolor='black')

# Add percentage labels
for bar_group in ax.containers:
    for bar in bar_group:
        h = bar.get_height()
        if h >= 0.05:
            ax.text(
                bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2,
                bar.get_y() + h / 2,
                f'{h:.0%}',
                ha='center', va='center',
                color='firebrick', fontweight='bold', fontsize=6
            )

ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(PercentFormatter(xmax=1))
ax.set_title('Stacked Bar Charts', fontsize=19, fontweight='bold')
ax.set_xlabel('Field of Study', fontsize=18)
ax.set_ylabel('Percent', fontsize=17)
ax.tick_params(axis='x', labelrotation=0, labelsize=17)
ax.tick_params(axis='y', labelsize=17)
ax.legend(title='Likelihood to Apply', fontsize=18, title_fontsize=17,
          bbox_to_anchor=(1.01, 1), loc='upper left')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```
:::

Box and violin plots, along with stacked bar charts, show descriptively how academic performance and field of study relate to a student's likelihood of applying to graduate school.

- Students who are `Very High` likelihood to apply tend to have the highest GPAs on average, with a median GPA visibly higher than those in the `Low` or `Medium` categories.
- The mean GPA, represented by the red diamonds in the violin plots, shows a consistent upward trend as the level of intent increases, suggesting a positive association between academic performance and likelihood to apply to graduate school. 
- While there is considerable overlap in the GPA distributions of the `Low` and `Medium` groups, the `Very High` group shows a distinct shift toward the higher end of the GPA scale.
- Differences in the distribution of `likelihood_to_apply` across fields of study suggest that a student's undergraduate discipline may influence their propensity to pursue graduate education.

Next, we examine how `GRE_score` relates to `likelihood_to_apply` using the same side-by-side boxplot and violin plot approach.

::: {.panel-tabset}

## R Code

```{.r}
college_data_gre_boxplots <- college_data |>
  ggplot(aes(likelihood_to_apply, GRE_score)) +
  geom_boxplot(aes(fill = likelihood_to_apply)) +
  labs(y = "GRE Score", x = "Likelihood to Apply to Graduate School") +
  ggtitle("Side-by-Side Boxplots") +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 19, face = "bold"),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 17),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 18),
    legend.position = "none"
  ) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette="BuPu")

college_data_gre_violin <- college_data |>
  ggplot(aes(likelihood_to_apply, GRE_score)) +
  geom_violin(aes(fill = likelihood_to_apply)) +
  labs(y = "GRE Score", x = "Likelihood to Apply to Graduate School") +
  ggtitle("Side-by-Side Violin Plots") +
  stat_summary(
    fun = mean, colour = "red", geom = "point",
    shape = 18, size = 5
  ) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 19, face = "bold"),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 17),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 18),
    legend.position = "none"
  ) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette="BuPu")

plot_grid(college_data_gre_boxplots, college_data_gre_violin, nrow = 1)
```

## Python Code

```{.py}
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 6))

# --- Boxplot ---
sns.boxplot(data=college_data, x='likelihood_to_apply', y='GRE_score', hue='likelihood_to_apply',
            palette='BuPu', ax=axes[0], legend=False)
axes[0].set_title('Side-by-Side Boxplots', fontsize=19, fontweight='bold')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Likelihood to Apply to Graduate School', fontsize=18)
axes[0].set_ylabel('GRE Score', fontsize=17)

# --- Violin Plot with mean point ---
sns.violinplot(data=college_data, x='likelihood_to_apply', y='GRE_score', hue='likelihood_to_apply',
               palette='BuPu', ax=axes[1], legend=False)
means = college_data.groupby('likelihood_to_apply')['GRE_score'].mean()
for i, (cat, mean_val) in enumerate(means.items()):
    axes[1].plot(i, mean_val, 'o', color='red', markersize=5, zorder=5)
axes[1].set_title('Side-by-Side Violin Plots', fontsize=19, fontweight='bold')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Likelihood to Apply to Graduate School', fontsize=18)
axes[1].set_ylabel('GRE Score', fontsize=17)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

:::

::: {.panel-tabset}

## R Output

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
college_data_gre_boxplots <- college_data |>
  ggplot(aes(likelihood_to_apply, GRE_score)) +
  geom_boxplot(aes(fill = likelihood_to_apply)) +
  labs(y = "GRE Score", x = "Likelihood to Apply to Graduate School") +
  ggtitle("Side-by-Side Boxplots") +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 19, face = "bold"),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 17),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 18),
    legend.position = "none"
  ) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette="BuPu")

college_data_gre_violin <- college_data |>
  ggplot(aes(likelihood_to_apply, GRE_score)) +
  geom_violin(aes(fill = likelihood_to_apply)) +
  labs(y = "GRE Score", x = "Likelihood to Apply to Graduate School") +
  ggtitle("Side-by-Side Violin Plots") +
  stat_summary(
    fun = mean, colour = "red", geom = "point",
    shape = 18, size = 5
  ) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 19, face = "bold"),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 17),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 18),
    legend.position = "none"
  ) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette="BuPu")

plot_grid(college_data_gre_boxplots, college_data_gre_violin, nrow = 1)
```

## Python Output

```{py}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 6))

# --- Boxplot ---
sns.boxplot(data=college_data, x='likelihood_to_apply', y='GRE_score', hue='likelihood_to_apply',
            palette='BuPu', ax=axes[0], legend=False)
axes[0].set_title('Side-by-Side Boxplots', fontsize=19, fontweight='bold')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Likelihood to Apply to Graduate School', fontsize=18)
axes[0].set_ylabel('GRE Score', fontsize=17)

# --- Violin Plot with mean point ---
sns.violinplot(data=college_data, x='likelihood_to_apply', y='GRE_score', hue='likelihood_to_apply',
               palette='BuPu', ax=axes[1], legend=False)
means = college_data.groupby('likelihood_to_apply')['GRE_score'].mean()
for i, (cat, mean_val) in enumerate(means.items()):
    axes[1].plot(i, mean_val, 'o', color='red', markersize=5, zorder=5)
axes[1].set_title('Side-by-Side Violin Plots', fontsize=19, fontweight='bold')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Likelihood to Apply to Graduate School', fontsize=18)
axes[1].set_ylabel('GRE Score', fontsize=17)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

:::

The GRE score plots mirror the structure of the GPA plots, allowing us to assess whether standardized test performance follows a similar pattern across likelihood-to-apply categories.

- The `Very High` group stands clearly apart from the other three, with a mean GRE score of approximately 154.3 — roughly 6 to 8 points higher than the `Low` (148.6), `Medium` (146.5), and `High` (148.0) groups. This separation is visible in both the boxplot and violin plot as a distinct upward shift in the distribution.
- Unlike GPA, GRE score does **not** increase monotonically across the ordered categories. The `Medium` group has the lowest mean GRE score of all four groups, and the `Low`, `Medium`, and `High` groups show substantially overlapping distributions. This suggests that GRE score may primarily distinguish students in the `Very High` category rather than tracking the full ordinal gradient.
- The `Low` group exhibits the widest spread in GRE scores (standard deviation ≈ 6.7), while the `High` group is the most tightly clustered (standard deviation ≈ 5.0), indicating greater homogeneity in standardized test performance among students who are likely — but not extremely likely — to apply.

## Data Modeling

We have been discussing the advantageous of using a different model in the presence of an **ordinal response variable**. In this section, we go over the details of data modeling for an **ordinal response variable** with a set of discrete or continuous explanatory variables using an ordinal logistic regression model. This type of modeling is the most frequent and popular model for ordinal responses. 

To present the details of the modeling framework, we begin with the concept of **cumulative logits**. We have already encountered the logit function in the context of binary logistic regression. Recall that in logistic regression, the logit function is applied to the odds of an event. Specifically, if 

$$
p = P(Y = 1 |X_1, X_2, ..., X_k)
$$

then the odds of the event $Y=1$ given the predictors is:

$$
\frac{P(Y = 1 \mid X_1, ..., X_k)}{P(Y = 0 \mid X_1, ..., X_k)} = \frac{p}{1-p}
$$

and the logit is defined as the logarithm of these odds, i.e. $log(\frac{p}{1-p})$. In logistic regression modeling we assume that this log-odds is a linear function of the predictors:

$$
log(\frac{p}{1-p}) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2X_2 + \ldots + \beta_k X_k
$$

This idea and formulation is the basis for extending logistic regression modeling for an outcome with two categories to ordinal outcomes where there are more than two categories and the order matters as well. In the ordinal case, instead of modeling the **log-odds of a single binary event**, we model **the cumulative logits** which compare the probability of being at or below a given category to the probability of being above that category. 

**Notation:** To avoid lengthy formulas, we introduce compact notation that will be used consistently throughout this chapter of book. In expressions such as $P(Y = 1 \mid X_1, ..., X_k)$ the conditioning is on all explanatory variables. Instead of repeatedly writing all predictors, we define:  

$$
\underline{X} = (X_1,...,X_k)
$$ 

and write;

$$
P(Y = 1 \mid \underline{X})
$$

This notation simplifies expressions and improves readability. Let's continue!

Imagine your ordered response variable have $J$ different categories where $J \ge 3$. We start with the category ordering by formally defining the **logits of cumulative probabilities** as follows. Let:

$$
P(Y \leq j \mid \underline{X}) = p_1(\underline{X}) + p_2(\underline{X}) + \ldots + p_j(\underline{X}) \quad \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, J-1
$$

be the cumulative probability. The **logits of cumulative probabilities** are defined as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{logit}(P(Y \leq j \mid \underline{X})) = \log( \frac{P(Y \leq j \mid \underline{X})}{ 1 - P(Y \leq j \mid \underline{X})} ) \\
&= \log( \frac{P(Y = 1 \mid \underline{X}) + P(Y = 2 \mid \underline{X}) + \ldots + P(Y = j \mid \underline{X})}{ P(Y = j+1 \mid \underline{X}) + P(Y = j+2 \mid \underline{X}) + \ldots + P(Y = J \mid \underline{X}) } )
\end{aligned}
$$

Perfect! We have defined our **cumulative logit odds here**! At this point, an important modeling decision must be addressed. When working with cumulative logits in ordinal logistic regression, there are two primary modeling approaches based on different assumptions about the relationship between the predictors and the response categories. These two primary modeling approaches are 1) the proportional odds assumption and 2) the non-proportional odds assumption. 

The proportional odds model assumes that the effect of each explanatory variable is constant across all cumulative logits, meaning that the regression coefficients do not depend on the category $j$. In contrast, the non-proportional odds model allows the effects of the explanatory variables to vary across cumulative splits of the response variable. In the following sections, we introduce these two approaches in detail and discuss their interpretation and practical implications. Later in this chapter we explain how you can verify these assumptions while fitting the model.


### The Proportional Odds Modeling

Up until now we established that for an ordinal response variable with $J$ ordered categories, we defined the cumulative logits as:

$$
\text{logit}(P(Y \le j \mid \underline{X})) = \frac{ P(Y \le j \mid \underline{X}) }{ P(Y \ge j+1 \mid \underline{X}) }
$$

for all values of $j = 1, 2, \ldots, J-1$. This means that for an outcome with $J$ categories, we have $J-1$ cumulative logits. A natural question now arises: **how should these cumulative logits depend on the explanatory variables?** The proportional odds model assumes that each cumulative logit is a linear function of the predictors, with the important restriction that the regression coefficients are the same across all cumulative logits. The model is written as:

$$
\text{logit}( P(Y \le j \mid \underline{X}) ) = \alpha_j + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + ... + \beta_k X_k
$$

for all values of $j = 1, 2, \ldots, J-1$. Note that in this formula:

- $\alpha_j$ is a category-specific intercept and depends on the category $j$,
- $\beta_1, ..., \beta_k$ are regression coefficients that do not depend on $j$. 

The essential assumption is that the effect of each explanatory variable on the log-odds is constant across all cumulative splits of the response variable. That is, the slope parameters remain the same whether we compare:

- category 1 versus higher categories  
- categories 1–2 versus higher categories  
- $\vdots$  
- categories 1 through $J-1$ versus higher categories

and in a more mathematical framework, that is:

- $P(Y \le 1 \mid \underline{X})$ versus $P(Y > 1 \mid \underline{X})$
- $P(Y \le 2 \mid \underline{X})$ versus $P(Y > 2 \mid \underline{X})$
- $\vdots$
- $P(Y \le J-1 \mid \underline{X})$ versus $P(Y > J-1 \mid \underline{X})$


This property of coefficients across cumulative logits is known as **the proportional odds assumption**.


### The Non-proportional Odds Modeling

While the proportional odds model assumes that the effect of each predictor is the same across all cumulative logits, this assumption may not always hold in practice. When the effect of one or more explanatory variables varies across the cumulative splits of the ordinal response, we use a non-proportional odds model.

Formally speaking, the non-proportional odds model allows the regression coefficients to depend on the cumulative category $j$ as shown below:

$$
\text{logit}( P(Y \le j \mid \underline{X}) ) = \alpha_j + \beta_{1j} X_1 + \beta_{2j} X_2 + \ldots + \beta_{kj} X_k
$$

In this model:

- $\alpha_j$ is the category-specific intercept (the same as before),
- $\beta_{1j}, \beta_{2j}, \ldots , \beta_{kj}$ are category-specific regression coefficients, meaning that the effect of each explanatory variable can differ across cumulative logits.

At the first glance this seems to be a great flexibility for modeling but this flexibility comes at a cost: **the number of parameters increases with the number of cumulative splits**, making the model more complex and potentially harder to interpret. However, it provides a more accurate representation when the proportional odds assumption is not valid. We will discuss how to assess these assumptions later in this chapter.


To help us understand the differences in assumption visually, we have the following plot. The figure below visualizes four cumulative probabilities $P(Y <= 1 \mid X)$, $P(Y <= 2 \mid X)$, $P(Y <= 3 \mid X)$, and $P(Y <= 4 \mid X)$ on the y-axis plotted as a logistic curve against one explanatory variable $X$. Notice that all curves have the same slope (same amount of steepness) but different intercepts. This parallel structure shows the proportional odds assumption: the effect of $X$ on the log-odds scale (which is the coefficient of $X$) is the same across all cumulative logits. In other words, increasing $X$ shifts each cumulative logit by the same amount regardless of which categorical group $j$ we consider. Note that the intercepts in this model are $\alpha_1 = 4$, $\alpha_2 = 5.5$, $\alpha_3 = 6.3$, $\alpha_4 = 10$. The coefficient which measures the effect of $X$ on log-odds is $\beta=12$ and the same for all cumulative probabilities. Thus, the visual parallelism in this plot provides an intuitive way to understand what proportional odds means geometrically


```{r echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Borrowed from DSCI 562 lecture notes written by Alexi
x_grid <- seq(-0.5, 3, length.out = 1200)
a_vals <- c("k = 4" = 10, "k = 3" = 6.3, "k = 2" = 5.5, "k = 1" = 4)

df <- map_dfr(names(a_vals), \(nm) {
  a <- a_vals[[nm]]
  tibble(
    x = x_grid,
    y = plogis(a - 12 * x),
    curve = nm
  )
}) |>
  mutate(curve = factor(curve, levels = names(a_vals)))

eq_df <- tibble(
  curve = factor(names(a_vals), levels = names(a_vals)),
  x = 3.1,
  y = c(0.95, 0.70, 0.45, 0.15),
  lab = c(
    "P(Y <= 4 ~ '|' ~ X)==frac(e^{10-12*x},1+e^{10-12*x})",
    "P(Y <= 3 ~ '|' ~ X)==frac(e^{6.3-12*x},1+e^{6.3-12*x})",
    "P(Y <= 2 ~ '|' ~ X)==frac(e^{5.5-12*x},1+e^{5.5-12*x})",
    "P(Y <= 1 ~ '|' ~ X)==frac(e^{4-12*x},1+e^{4-12*x})"
  )
)

cb_pal <- c(
  "k = 4" = "#0072B2",
  "k = 3" = "#D55E00",
  "k = 2" = "#009E73",
  "k = 1" = "#CC79A7"
)

ggplot(df, aes(x, y, color = curve)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_text(
    data = eq_df,
    aes(x = x, y = y, label = lab, color = curve),
    hjust = 1, vjust = 0.5, parse = TRUE,
    size = 7.5,         
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = cb_pal) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-0.5, 3), ylim = c(0, 1.05)) +
  labs(x = "x", y = "Cumulative Probability") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title = element_text(size = 24),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 17),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 21)
  )
```


If the proportional odds assumption were violated, these curves would no longer be parallel on the logit scale; instead, their slopes would differ, indicating that the effect of $X$ changes depending on which cumulative split of the ordinal outcome is being modeled. The following figure is an example of proportional odds assumption being violated:

```{r echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Non-proportional odds visualization

x_grid <- seq(-0.5, 3, length.out = 1200)

# Different intercepts and coefficients
param_vals <- tribble(
  ~curve,   ~a,   ~b,
  "k = 4",  10,  -12,
  "k = 3",  6.3,  -9,
  "k = 2",  5.5,  -6,
  "k = 1",  4,    -3
)

df_np <- param_vals |>
  mutate(data = map2(a, b, ~tibble(
    x = x_grid,
    y = plogis(.x + .y * x_grid)
  ))) |>
  unnest(data) |>
  mutate(curve = factor(curve, levels = param_vals$curve))

eq_df_np <- param_vals |>
  mutate(
    x = 3.1,
    y = c(0.95, 0.70, 0.45, 0.15),
    lab = paste0(
      "P(Y <= ", 4:1, " ~ '|' ~ X)==",
      "frac(e^{", a, ifelse(b >= 0, "+", ""), b, "*x},",
      "1+e^{", a, ifelse(b >= 0, "+", ""), b, "*x})"
    )
  )

cb_pal <- c(
  "k = 4" = "#0072B2",
  "k = 3" = "#D55E00",
  "k = 2" = "#009E73",
  "k = 1" = "#CC79A7"
)

ggplot(df_np, aes(x, y, color = curve)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_text(
    data = eq_df_np,
    aes(x = x, y = y, label = lab, color = curve),
    hjust = 1, vjust = 0.5, parse = TRUE,
    size = 7.5,
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = cb_pal) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-0.5, 3), ylim = c(0, 1.05)) +
  labs(x = "x", y = "Cumulative Probability") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title = element_text(size = 24),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 17),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 21)
  )
```


## Estimation

We now turn to the question of how the parameters of the ordinal logistic regression model are estimated from data. We summarise the key estimation steps in mathematical terms below, working with one observation at a time before generalizing to the full sample.

Recall that the proportional odds model for an ordinal response $Y$ with $J$ ordered categories and $K$ predictors is:

$$
\text{logit}(P(Y \le j \mid \underline{X})) = \alpha_j - (\beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \ldots + \beta_K X_K), \quad j = 1, \ldots, J-1,
$$

where $\alpha_1 < \alpha_2 < \cdots < \alpha_{J-1}$ are the category-specific intercepts (also called **thresholds** or **cutpoints**) and $\beta_1, \ldots, \beta_K$ are the shared slope coefficients. Note the sign convention: the predictor terms are subtracted from the intercept so that a positive $\beta_k$ corresponds to higher values of $X_k$ shifting probability mass toward higher categories. This sign convention is standard in R's `MASS::polr` and in most textbooks, and it means $\alpha_j$ increases with $j$ to preserve the ordering of cumulative probabilities.

The total number of parameters to estimate is $(J - 1) + K$: one threshold per cumulative split, plus one slope per predictor.

### Category Probabilities from the Cumulative Model

From the cumulative probabilities $P(Y \le j \mid \underline{X})$, we recover the **category-specific probabilities** by differencing:

$$
p_j(\underline{X}) = P(Y = j \mid \underline{X}) = P(Y \le j \mid \underline{X}) - P(Y \le j-1 \mid \underline{X}),
$$

where by convention $P(Y \le 0 \mid \underline{X}) = 0$ and $P(Y \le J \mid \underline{X}) = 1$. Substituting the logistic cumulative probability $F(\cdot) = \frac{e^{(\cdot)}}{1 + e^{(\cdot)}}$, this gives:

$$
p_j(\underline{X}) = \frac{e^{\alpha_j - \boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \underline{X}}}{1 + e^{\alpha_j - \boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \underline{X}}} - \frac{e^{\alpha_{j-1} - \boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \underline{X}}}{1 + e^{\alpha_{j-1} - \boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \underline{X}}}.
$$

These category probabilities are guaranteed to be non-negative (because $\alpha_j > \alpha_{j-1}$ ensures each difference is positive) and sum to one across $j = 1, \ldots, J$.

### The Log-Likelihood Function

Let $y_{i,j} = 1$ if the $i$th individual falls in category $j$, and $y_{i,j} = 0$ otherwise. The likelihood function for the ordinal logistic regression model is:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\alpha}, \boldsymbol{\beta} \mid y) = \prod_{i=1}^n \prod_{j=1}^J p_j(\underline{X}_i)^{y_{i,j}},
$$

where $\boldsymbol{\alpha} = (\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_{J-1})^\top$ is the vector of thresholds and $\boldsymbol{\beta} = (\beta_1, \ldots, \beta_K)^\top$ is the vector of shared slopes. This is a **multinomial likelihood** evaluated at the ordinal-model category probabilities, rather than at freely estimated per-category probabilities.

Taking the logarithm yields the log-likelihood:

$$
\ell(\boldsymbol{\alpha}, \boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{j=1}^J y_{i,j} \log p_j(\underline{X}_i).
$$

There is no closed-form solution for $\hat{\boldsymbol{\alpha}}$ and $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ that maximizes $\ell$, so we rely on iterative numerical optimization, typically the **Newton–Raphson** method.

### The Newton–Raphson Method

Let $\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_{J-1}, \beta_1, \ldots, \beta_K)^\top \in \mathbb{R}^{(J-1)+K}$ collect all parameters. The Newton–Raphson procedure iterates using the score function and Hessian matrix of the log-likelihood.

**Score function.** The partial derivative of the log-likelihood with respect to each threshold $\alpha_j$ is:

$$
\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \alpha_j} = \sum_{i=1}^n \left[ \frac{y_{i,j}}{p_j(\underline{X}_i)} - \frac{y_{i,j+1}}{p_{j+1}(\underline{X}_i)} \right] f(\alpha_j - \boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \underline{X}_i),
$$

where $f(\cdot) = F(\cdot)(1 - F(\cdot))$ is the logistic density. The partial derivative with respect to each shared slope $\beta_k$ is:

$$
\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \beta_k} = -\sum_{i=1}^n X_{i,k} \sum_{j=1}^{J} y_{i,j} \left[ \frac{f(\alpha_j - \boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \underline{X}_i)}{p_j(\underline{X}_i)} - \frac{f(\alpha_{j-1} - \boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \underline{X}_i)}{p_j(\underline{X}_i)} \right].
$$

The key difference from the multinomial case is that the slopes $\beta_k$ appear in **all** $J-1$ cumulative logits simultaneously, so their score components aggregate information across every cumulative split.

**Hessian matrix.** The Hessian $H(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ consists of second-order partial derivatives of $\ell$. Because the shared slopes couple all cumulative logits together, the Hessian is a dense $(J-1+K) \times (J-1+K)$ matrix — unlike the block-diagonal structure seen in multinomial logistic regression. In software this is handled automatically.

**Iteration.** The Newton–Raphson update at step $t$ is:

$$
\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t+1)} \leftarrow \boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t)} - \left[ H\!\left(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t)}\right) \right]^{-1} S\!\left(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t)}\right),
$$

where $S(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ is the stacked score vector. The algorithm is initialized at $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(0)} = \mathbf{0}$ (or a data-driven starting point) and repeats until changes in $\ell$ or $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ fall below a convergence tolerance.

The full Newton–Raphson/IRLS procedure is:

1. Initialize $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(0)} = \mathbf{0}$ (stacked thresholds and slopes).
2. Repeat until convergence:
   - Compute the linear predictors $\alpha_j - \boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \underline{X}_i$ for all $i$ and $j$.
   - Compute cumulative probabilities $P(Y \le j \mid \underline{X}_i)$ and category probabilities $p_j(\underline{X}_i)$.
   - Compute the log-likelihood $\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t)})$, score $S(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t)})$, and Hessian $H(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t)})$.
   - Update $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t+1)} \leftarrow \boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t)} - [H(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t)})]^{-1} S(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t)})$.
   - Check convergence.

### Fitting the Proportional Odds Model

We now fit the proportional odds model to the college dataset using `likelihood_to_apply` as the ordinal response with four levels (`Low < Medium < High < Very High`) and `GPA`, `GRE_score`, and `field_of_study` as regressors. In R, we use `polr()` from the `MASS` package; in Python, we use `OrderedModel` from `statsmodels`.

::: {.panel-tabset}

## R Code

```{.r}
library(MASS)

# Ensure likelihood_to_apply is an ordered factor with correct level order
college_data$likelihood_to_apply <- factor(
  college_data$likelihood_to_apply,
  levels = c("Low", "Medium", "High", "Very High"),
  ordered = TRUE
)

# Fit the proportional odds model
# Note: polr() uses the sign convention logit P(Y <= j) = alpha_j - beta * X
ordinal_fit <- polr(
  likelihood_to_apply ~ GPA + GRE_score + field_of_study,
  data = college_data,
  Hess = TRUE   # return the Hessian for inference
)

summary(ordinal_fit)
```

## Python Code

```{.python}
from statsmodels.miscmodels.ordinal_model import OrderedModel

# Ensure likelihood_to_apply is an ordered categorical
ordered_categories = ["Low", "Medium", "High", "Very High"]
college_data["likelihood_to_apply"] = pd.Categorical(
    college_data["likelihood_to_apply"],
    categories=ordered_categories,
    ordered=True
)

# Fit the proportional odds model (logit link)
ordinal_fit = OrderedModel(
    college_data["likelihood_to_apply"],
    college_data[["GPA", "GRE_score", "field_of_study"]],
    distr="logit"
)
ordinal_result = ordinal_fit.fit(method="bfgs", disp=False)
print(ordinal_result.summary())
```

:::

::: {.panel-tabset}

## R Output

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
library(MASS)

college_data$likelihood_to_apply <- factor(
  college_data$likelihood_to_apply,
  levels = c("Low", "Medium", "High", "Very High"),
  ordered = TRUE
)

ordinal_fit <- polr(
  likelihood_to_apply ~ GPA + GRE_score + field_of_study,
  data = college_data,
  Hess = TRUE
)

summary(ordinal_fit)
```

## Python Output

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
from statsmodels.miscmodels.ordinal_model import OrderedModel

ordered_categories = ["Low", "Medium", "High", "Very High"]
college_data["likelihood_to_apply"] = pd.Categorical(
    college_data["likelihood_to_apply"],
    categories=ordered_categories,
    ordered=True
)

ordinal_fit = OrderedModel(
    college_data["likelihood_to_apply"],
    college_data[["GPA", "GRE_score", "field_of_study"]],
    distr="logit"
)
ordinal_result = ordinal_fit.fit(method="bfgs", disp=False)
print(ordinal_result.summary())
```

:::

The output reports two blocks of estimates. The first block contains the shared slope coefficients $\hat{\beta}_1, \ldots, \hat{\beta}_K$ — one per regressor — along with their standard errors and $t$-values. The second block contains the estimated thresholds $\hat{\alpha}_1 < \hat{\alpha}_2 < \hat{\alpha}_3$, which correspond to the three cumulative splits `Low|Medium`, `Medium|High`, and `High|Very High`. The fact that the thresholds are strictly increasing confirms that the monotonicity constraint on cumulative probabilities is satisfied by the fitted model.

In the case study model with regressors $X_{\texttt{GPA}}$, $X_{\texttt{GRE}}$, and the `field-of-study` dummy variables $Z_{\texttt{field}}$, the fitted proportional odds model can be written as:

$$
\text{logit}\!\left(P(Y \le j \mid \underline{X})\right) = \hat{\alpha}_j - \left(\hat{\beta}_{\texttt{GPA}}\, X_{\texttt{GPA}} + \hat{\beta}_{\texttt{GRE}}\, X_{\texttt{GRE}} + \hat{\beta}_{\texttt{field}}\, Z_{\texttt{field}}\right), \quad j = 1, 2, 3,
$$

where each $\hat{\alpha}_j$ is read from the thresholds block of the output and each $\hat{\beta}$ from the coefficients block.

### Fitting the Non-Proportional Odds Model

When the proportional odds assumption is violated for one or more regressors, we relax the shared-slope constraint and allow each cumulative logit to have its own set of regression coefficients. Recall the non-proportional odds model introduced in the Data Modeling section:

$$
\text{logit}( P(Y \le j \mid \underline{X}) ) = \alpha_j - (\beta_{1j} X_1 + \beta_{2j} X_2 + \ldots + \beta_{Kj} X_K), \quad j = 1, \ldots, J-1,
$$

where every slope $\beta_{kj}$ now depends on the cumulative split $j$. The total number of parameters grows from $(J-1) + K$ in the proportional odds model to $(J-1) \times (1 + K)$ in the fully non-proportional model — one intercept and $K$ slopes for each of the $J-1$ cumulative logits. For our case study with $J = 4$ categories and $K$ regressors, this is a substantial increase, which is why the non-proportional model should only be preferred when the data clearly support it.

**Log-likelihood under non-proportional odds.** The likelihood has the same multinomial form as before:

$$
\ell(\boldsymbol{\alpha}, \boldsymbol{B}) = \sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{j=1}^J y_{i,j} \log p_j(\underline{X}_i),
$$

but now the category probabilities $p_j(\underline{X}_i)$ are computed from cumulative probabilities that use split-specific slopes $\boldsymbol{\beta}_j = (\beta_{1j}, \ldots, \beta_{Kj})^\top$:

$$
P(Y \le j \mid \underline{X}_i) = \frac{e^{\alpha_j - \boldsymbol{\beta}_j^\top \underline{X}_i}}{1 + e^{\alpha_j - \boldsymbol{\beta}_j^\top \underline{X}_i}}.
$$

The parameter matrix $\boldsymbol{B} = [\boldsymbol{\beta}_1 \mid \ldots \mid \boldsymbol{\beta}_{J-1}]$ replaces the single shared vector $\boldsymbol{\beta}$. Newton–Raphson proceeds identically to the proportional odds case, but the score and Hessian are now computed with respect to all $(J-1)(K+1)$ free parameters. Because the cumulative logits are no longer coupled through a single $\boldsymbol{\beta}$, the Hessian has a more favourable block structure — in principle, each cumulative logit can be optimized independently — though software typically optimizes all parameters jointly.

**A practical middle ground: the partial proportional odds model.** In many applications, the proportional odds assumption holds for some regressors but not others. Rather than defaulting to either extreme, a **partial proportional odds model** constrains the slopes for well-behaved regressors to be shared across cutpoints, while freeing the slopes for regressors that violate the assumption. This is the most common approach in practice and is supported by the `VGAM` package in R via the `vglm()` function with the `cumulative` family.

In R we fit the fully non-proportional model using `vglm()` from the `VGAM` package. Setting `parallel = FALSE` removes the proportional odds constraint entirely. Setting `parallel = TRUE` would recover the proportional odds model, and passing a named logical vector (e.g. `parallel = c(GPA = TRUE, GRE_score = FALSE)`) fits the partial proportional odds model.

In Python, `statsmodels` does not currently provide a built-in non-proportional ordinal model. A practical approach is to reframe the $J-1$ cumulative binary splits as separate binary logistic regressions — one model per cutpoint — and fit each independently using `LogisticRegression` or `sm.Logit`.

::: {.panel-tabset}

## R Code

```{.r}
library(VGAM)

# Fully non-proportional odds model (parallel = FALSE relaxes the constraint
# for all predictors; each cumulative logit gets its own set of slopes)
ordinal_fit_np <- vglm(
  likelihood_to_apply ~ GPA + GRE_score + field_of_study,
  family = cumulative(link = "logitlink", parallel = FALSE, reverse = FALSE),
  data = college_data
)

summary(ordinal_fit_np)
```

## Python Code

```{.python}
# Fit J-1 = 3 separate binary logistic regressions, one per cumulative split.
# Each models P(Y <= j) vs P(Y > j) for j = 1, 2, 3.

from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
import numpy as np

cats      = ["Low", "Medium", "High", "Very High"]
X_np      = pd.get_dummies(
    college_data[["GPA", "GRE_score", "field_of_study"]],
    drop_first=True
).astype(float)
y_ord     = college_data["likelihood_to_apply"].cat.codes  # 0,1,2,3

cutpoints = ["Low|Medium", "Medium|High", "High|Very High"]
np_models = {}

for j, label in enumerate(cutpoints):
    # binary target: 1 if Y <= j, 0 if Y > j
    y_bin = (y_ord <= j).astype(int)
    clf   = LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, random_state=0)
    clf.fit(X_np, y_bin)
    np_models[label] = clf
    coefs = dict(zip(X_np.columns, clf.coef_[0]))
    print(f"\nCutpoint {label}  (intercept = {clf.intercept_[0]:.3f})")
    for var, coef in coefs.items():
        print(f"  {var}: {coef:.3f}")
```

:::

::: {.panel-tabset}

## R Output

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
library(VGAM)

ordinal_fit_np <- vglm(
  likelihood_to_apply ~ GPA + GRE_score + field_of_study,
  family = cumulative(link = "logitlink", parallel = FALSE, reverse = FALSE),
  data = college_data
)

summary(ordinal_fit_np)
```

## Python Output

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
import numpy as np

cats  = ["Low", "Medium", "High", "Very High"]
X_np  = pd.get_dummies(
    college_data[["GPA", "GRE_score", "field_of_study"]],
    drop_first=True
).astype(float)
y_ord = college_data["likelihood_to_apply"].cat.codes

cutpoints = ["Low|Medium", "Medium|High", "High|Very High"]
np_models = {}

for j, label in enumerate(cutpoints):
    y_bin = (y_ord <= j).astype(int)
    clf   = LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, random_state=0)
    clf.fit(X_np, y_bin)
    np_models[label] = clf
    coefs = dict(zip(X_np.columns, clf.coef_[0]))
    print(f"\nCutpoint {label}  (intercept = {clf.intercept_[0]:.3f})")
    for var, coef in coefs.items():
        print(f"  {var}: {coef:.3f}")
```

:::

The R output from `vglm()` displays a separate row of coefficient estimates for each regressor at each of the $J-1$ cumulative logits. If the proportional odds assumption held perfectly, these slope estimates would be identical across cutpoints. Marked differences between them, especially relative to their standard errors, are an informal signal that the assumption may be violated. The "curves approach" introduced next provides a complementary visual way to assess this.

### Checking the Proportional Odds Assumption

In the Data Modeling section, we introduced a geometric approach to understand the proportional odds assumption: when the assumption holds, the $J-1$ cumulative probability curves plotted against a continuous regressor are **parallel** on the logit scale (same slope, shifting intercepts). When the assumption is violated, the curves cross or fan out, reflecting different slopes at different cutpoints.

We now apply this approach directly to the fitted models on the college data. For each continuous regressor (`GPA` and `GRE_score`), we plot the $J-1 = 3$ estimated cumulative probability curves — $\hat{P}(Y \le j \mid X)$ for $j = 1, 2, 3$ — while holding all other regressors at their sample means or reference levels. Under the proportional odds model the curves are constrained to be parallel by construction. Under the non-proportional odds model they are free to differ in slope at each cutpoint.

Comparing the two sets of curves side-by-side gives a direct visual test: if the non-proportional curves look nearly as parallel as the proportional ones, the simpler proportional odds model is adequate. If the non-proportional curves diverge noticeably from one another, the proportional odds assumption is questionable for that regressor.

::: {.panel-tabset}

## R Code

```{.r}
library(MASS)
library(VGAM)
library(tidyverse)
library(cowplot)

# ── Helper: predicted cumulative curves for polr (proportional odds) ──────────
pred_curves_polr <- function(fit, predictor, data, n_grid = 200) {
  x_seq   <- seq(min(data[[predictor]]), max(data[[predictor]]), length.out = n_grid)
  newdata <- data[rep(1, n_grid), ]
  # Set all numeric predictors to their mean, factors to reference level
  for (v in names(newdata)) {
    if (is.numeric(newdata[[v]])) newdata[[v]] <- mean(data[[v]])
    if (is.factor(newdata[[v]]))  newdata[[v]] <- levels(newdata[[v]])[1]
  }
  newdata[[predictor]] <- x_seq
  probs <- predict(fit, newdata = newdata, type = "probs")
  # Convert category probs to cumulative probs
  cum_probs <- t(apply(probs, 1, cumsum))
  lvls <- levels(data$likelihood_to_apply)
  cum_probs <- cum_probs[, -ncol(cum_probs), drop = FALSE]  # drop P(Y <= J) = 1
  colnames(cum_probs) <- paste0("P(Y \u2264 ", lvls[-length(lvls)], ")")
  as.data.frame(cum_probs) |>
    mutate(x = x_seq, model = "Proportional Odds") |>
    pivot_longer(starts_with("P(Y"), names_to = "cutpoint", values_to = "prob")
}

# ── Helper: predicted cumulative curves for vglm (non-proportional odds) ──────
pred_curves_vglm <- function(fit, predictor, data, n_grid = 200) {
  x_seq   <- seq(min(data[[predictor]]), max(data[[predictor]]), length.out = n_grid)
  newdata <- data[rep(1, n_grid), ]
  for (v in names(newdata)) {
    if (is.numeric(newdata[[v]])) newdata[[v]] <- mean(data[[v]])
    if (is.factor(newdata[[v]]))  newdata[[v]] <- levels(newdata[[v]])[1]
  }
  newdata[[predictor]] <- x_seq
  cum_probs <- predictvglm(fit, newdata = newdata, type = "response")
  cum_probs <- t(apply(cum_probs, 1, cumsum))
  lvls <- levels(data$likelihood_to_apply)
  cum_probs <- cum_probs[, -ncol(cum_probs), drop = FALSE]
  colnames(cum_probs) <- paste0("P(Y \u2264 ", lvls[-length(lvls)], ")")
  as.data.frame(cum_probs) |>
    mutate(x = x_seq, model = "Non-Proportional Odds") |>
    pivot_longer(starts_with("P(Y"), names_to = "cutpoint", values_to = "prob")
}

# ── Build and combine curves for GPA ─────────────────────────────────────────
gpa_po  <- pred_curves_polr(ordinal_fit,    "GPA", college_data)
gpa_np  <- pred_curves_vglm(ordinal_fit_np, "GPA", college_data)
gpa_all <- bind_rows(gpa_po, gpa_np) |>
  mutate(model = factor(model, levels = c("Proportional Odds", "Non-Proportional Odds")))

p_gpa <- ggplot(gpa_all, aes(x = x, y = prob, colour = cutpoint)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  facet_wrap(~ model) +
  labs(x = "GPA", y = "Cumulative Probability", colour = "Cutpoint",
       title = "GPA: Proportional vs. Non-Proportional Odds") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        plot.title = element_text(size = 15, face = "bold"),
        axis.text  = element_text(size = 13),
        axis.title = element_text(size = 14))

# ── Build and combine curves for GRE_score ───────────────────────────────────
gre_po  <- pred_curves_polr(ordinal_fit,    "GRE_score", college_data)
gre_np  <- pred_curves_vglm(ordinal_fit_np, "GRE_score", college_data)
gre_all <- bind_rows(gre_po, gre_np) |>
  mutate(model = factor(model, levels = c("Proportional Odds", "Non-Proportional Odds")))

p_gre <- ggplot(gre_all, aes(x = x, y = prob, colour = cutpoint)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  facet_wrap(~ model) +
  labs(x = "GRE Score", y = "Cumulative Probability", colour = "Cutpoint",
       title = "GRE Score: Proportional vs. Non-Proportional Odds") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        plot.title = element_text(size = 15, face = "bold"),
        axis.text  = element_text(size = 13),
        axis.title = element_text(size = 14))

plot_grid(p_gpa, p_gre, nrow = 2)
```

## Python Code

```{.python}
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from statsmodels.miscmodels.ordinal_model import OrderedModel
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression

cats = ["Low", "Medium", "High", "Very High"]
cutpoint_labels = ["P(Y ≤ Low)", "P(Y ≤ Medium)", "P(Y ≤ High)"]
colors = ["#CC79A7", "#009E73", "#D55E00"]

# Pre-compute mean values and dummy-encoded predictors
X_full = pd.get_dummies(
    college_data[["GPA", "GRE_score", "field_of_study"]], drop_first=True
).astype(float)
X_mean = X_full.mean()

def cumulative_curves_po(result, predictor_col, predictor_range, X_mean, X_full):
    """Cumulative curves from the proportional odds OrderedModel fit."""
    curves = []
    for xval in predictor_range:
        row = X_mean.copy()
        row[predictor_col] = xval
        p_cat = result.model.predict(result.params, exog=row.values.reshape(1, -1))[0]
        curves.append(np.cumsum(p_cat)[:-1])  # drop P(Y <= J) = 1
    return np.array(curves)   # shape (n_grid, J-1)

def cumulative_curves_np(np_models, predictor_col, predictor_range, X_mean, X_full):
    """Cumulative curves from the J-1 separately fitted binary logistic models."""
    curves = []
    for xval in predictor_range:
        row = X_mean.copy()
        row[predictor_col] = xval
        cum_probs = [m.predict_proba(row.values.reshape(1, -1))[0, 1]
                     for m in np_models.values()]
        curves.append(cum_probs)
    return np.array(curves)   # shape (n_grid, J-1)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(14, 10))
fig.suptitle("Proportional vs. Non-Proportional Odds: Cumulative Probability Curves",
             fontsize=15, fontweight="bold")

for row_idx, (predictor, xlabel) in enumerate([("GPA", "GPA"),
                                                ("GRE_score", "GRE Score")]):
    x_range = np.linspace(X_full[predictor].min(), X_full[predictor].max(), 200)

    curves_po = cumulative_curves_po(ordinal_result, predictor, x_range, X_mean, X_full)
    curves_np = cumulative_curves_np(np_models, predictor, x_range, X_mean, X_full)

    for model_idx, (curves, title) in enumerate(
        [(curves_po, "Proportional Odds"), (curves_np, "Non-Proportional Odds")]
    ):
        ax = axes[row_idx, model_idx]
        for j, (label, color) in enumerate(zip(cutpoint_labels, colors)):
            ax.plot(x_range, curves[:, j], color=color, linewidth=2, label=label)
        ax.set_xlabel(xlabel, fontsize=13)
        ax.set_ylabel("Cumulative Probability", fontsize=13)
        ax.set_title(f"{xlabel}: {title}", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
        ax.set_ylim(0, 1)
        ax.legend(fontsize=11)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

:::

::: {.panel-tabset}

## R Output

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
library(MASS)
library(VGAM)
library(tidyverse)
library(cowplot)

pred_curves_polr <- function(fit, predictor, data, n_grid = 200) {
  x_seq   <- seq(min(data[[predictor]]), max(data[[predictor]]), length.out = n_grid)
  newdata <- data[rep(1, n_grid), ]
  for (v in names(newdata)) {
    if (is.numeric(newdata[[v]])) newdata[[v]] <- mean(data[[v]])
    if (is.factor(newdata[[v]]))  newdata[[v]] <- levels(newdata[[v]])[1]
  }
  newdata[[predictor]] <- x_seq
  probs     <- predict(fit, newdata = newdata, type = "probs")
  cum_probs <- t(apply(probs, 1, cumsum))
  lvls      <- levels(data$likelihood_to_apply)
  cum_probs <- cum_probs[, -ncol(cum_probs), drop = FALSE]
  colnames(cum_probs) <- paste0("P(Y \u2264 ", lvls[-length(lvls)], ")")
  as.data.frame(cum_probs) |>
    mutate(x = x_seq, model = "Proportional Odds") |>
    pivot_longer(starts_with("P(Y"), names_to = "cutpoint", values_to = "prob")
}

pred_curves_vglm <- function(fit, predictor, data, n_grid = 200) {
  x_seq   <- seq(min(data[[predictor]]), max(data[[predictor]]), length.out = n_grid)
  newdata <- data[rep(1, n_grid), ]
  for (v in names(newdata)) {
    if (is.numeric(newdata[[v]])) newdata[[v]] <- mean(data[[v]])
    if (is.factor(newdata[[v]]))  newdata[[v]] <- levels(newdata[[v]])[1]
  }
  newdata[[predictor]] <- x_seq
  cum_probs <- predictvglm(fit, newdata = newdata, type = "response")
  cum_probs <- t(apply(cum_probs, 1, cumsum))
  lvls      <- levels(data$likelihood_to_apply)
  cum_probs <- cum_probs[, -ncol(cum_probs), drop = FALSE]
  colnames(cum_probs) <- paste0("P(Y \u2264 ", lvls[-length(lvls)], ")")
  as.data.frame(cum_probs) |>
    mutate(x = x_seq, model = "Non-Proportional Odds") |>
    pivot_longer(starts_with("P(Y"), names_to = "cutpoint", values_to = "prob")
}

gpa_po  <- pred_curves_polr(ordinal_fit,    "GPA", college_data)
gpa_np  <- pred_curves_vglm(ordinal_fit_np, "GPA", college_data)
gpa_all <- bind_rows(gpa_po, gpa_np) |>
  mutate(model = factor(model, levels = c("Proportional Odds", "Non-Proportional Odds")))

p_gpa <- ggplot(gpa_all, aes(x = x, y = prob, colour = cutpoint)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  facet_wrap(~ model) +
  labs(x = "GPA", y = "Cumulative Probability", colour = "Cutpoint",
       title = "GPA: Proportional vs. Non-Proportional Odds") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        plot.title = element_text(size = 15, face = "bold"),
        axis.text  = element_text(size = 13),
        axis.title = element_text(size = 14))

gre_po  <- pred_curves_polr(ordinal_fit,    "GRE_score", college_data)
gre_np  <- pred_curves_vglm(ordinal_fit_np, "GRE_score", college_data)
gre_all <- bind_rows(gre_po, gre_np) |>
  mutate(model = factor(model, levels = c("Proportional Odds", "Non-Proportional Odds")))

p_gre <- ggplot(gre_all, aes(x = x, y = prob, colour = cutpoint)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  facet_wrap(~ model) +
  labs(x = "GRE Score", y = "Cumulative Probability", colour = "Cutpoint",
       title = "GRE Score: Proportional vs. Non-Proportional Odds") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        plot.title = element_text(size = 15, face = "bold"),
        axis.text  = element_text(size = 13),
        axis.title = element_text(size = 14))

plot_grid(p_gpa, p_gre, nrow = 2)
```

## Python Output

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

cats = ["Low", "Medium", "High", "Very High"]
cutpoint_labels = ["P(Y ≤ Low)", "P(Y ≤ Medium)", "P(Y ≤ High)"]
colors = ["#CC79A7", "#009E73", "#D55E00"]

X_full = pd.get_dummies(
    college_data[["GPA", "GRE_score", "field_of_study"]], drop_first=True
).astype(float)
X_mean = X_full.mean()

def cumulative_curves_po(result, predictor_col, predictor_range, X_mean, X_full):
    curves = []
    for xval in predictor_range:
        row = X_mean.copy()
        row[predictor_col] = xval
        p_cat = result.model.predict(result.params, exog=row.values.reshape(1, -1))[0]
        curves.append(np.cumsum(p_cat)[:-1])
    return np.array(curves)

def cumulative_curves_np(np_models, predictor_col, predictor_range, X_mean, X_full):
    curves = []
    for xval in predictor_range:
        row = X_mean.copy()
        row[predictor_col] = xval
        cum_probs = [m.predict_proba(row.values.reshape(1, -1))[0, 1]
                     for m in np_models.values()]
        curves.append(cum_probs)
    return np.array(curves)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(14, 10))
fig.suptitle("Proportional vs. Non-Proportional Odds: Cumulative Probability Curves",
             fontsize=15, fontweight="bold")

for row_idx, (predictor, xlabel) in enumerate([("GPA", "GPA"),
                                                ("GRE_score", "GRE Score")]):
    x_range = np.linspace(X_full[predictor].min(), X_full[predictor].max(), 200)
    curves_po = cumulative_curves_po(ordinal_result, predictor, x_range, X_mean, X_full)
    curves_np = cumulative_curves_np(np_models, predictor, x_range, X_mean, X_full)

    for model_idx, (curves, title) in enumerate(
        [(curves_po, "Proportional Odds"), (curves_np, "Non-Proportional Odds")]
    ):
        ax = axes[row_idx, model_idx]
        for j, (label, color) in enumerate(zip(cutpoint_labels, colors)):
            ax.plot(x_range, curves[:, j], color=color, linewidth=2, label=label)
        ax.set_xlabel(xlabel, fontsize=13)
        ax.set_ylabel("Cumulative Probability", fontsize=13)
        ax.set_title(f"{xlabel}: {title}", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
        ax.set_ylim(0, 1)
        ax.legend(fontsize=11)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

:::

Each figure shows two panels side-by-side for a given predictor: the left panel displays the $J-1$ cumulative probability curves from the **proportional odds model**, and the right panel displays the same curves from the **non-proportional odds model**. The key question is whether the shapes of the curves in the right panel depart meaningfully from the parallel structure enforced by the left panel.

- **If the non-proportional curves look approximately parallel** — similar spacing and steepness across all three cutpoints — the proportional odds assumption is visually plausible and the simpler `polr()` model is preferred. The added complexity of the non-proportional model is not supported by the data.
- **If the non-proportional curves diverge noticeably** — crossing, fanning out, or showing different rates of change — the proportional odds assumption is questionable for that predictor. In that case, a **partial proportional odds model** (freeing only the offending predictor via `parallel = c(...)` in `vglm()`) is a principled middle ground.

## Goodness of Fit


## Inference

After we finish estimating the coefficients, we examine how uncertain they are and whether they differ meaningfully from zero. We get a variance estimate for each coefficient from the observed information matrix (the negative of the Hessian at the MLE solution). Taking the square roots of those diagonal variances gives standard errors. Each coefficient divided by its standard error gives an asymptotically Normal **Wald $z$-statistic** for testing whether the true effect is zero. Using the same standard errors we build confidence intervals: estimate $\pm$ (critical value $\times$ standard error). To make results easier to interpret, we exponentiate a coefficient (or its confidence limits) to obtain a **proportional odds ratio** — how the cumulative odds of being at or below any category threshold change for a one-unit increase in the predictor, holding other variables fixed.

A key advantage of the proportional odds model compared to the multinomial model is parsimony: because the proportional odds assumption constrains each predictor to a **single shared slope** across all $J - 1$ cumulative logits, there is exactly one odds ratio to interpret per predictor rather than one per outcome category. This single number summarises the predictor's effect on the entire ordinal scale.

We can determine whether a regressor is statistically associated with the cumulative log-odds through hypothesis testing for the parameters $\beta_k$. Define the Wald statistic $z_k$ as:

$$
z_k = \frac{\hat{\beta}_k}{\widehat{\text{se}}\!\left(\hat{\beta}_k\right)}
$$

and we test the hypotheses:

$$
\begin{gather*}
H_0: \beta_k = 0 \\
H_a: \beta_k \neq 0.
\end{gather*}
$$

Provided the sample size $n$ is large enough, $z_k$ follows an approximately Standard Normal distribution under $H_0$. Note that `polr()` in R labels this column `t value` in its summary output, but the inference is based on the Normal approximation.

The corresponding $p$-values for each $\hat{\beta}_k$ can be computed. The smaller the $p$-value, the stronger the evidence against $H_0$. As in previous regression models, we set a predetermined significance level (usually $\alpha = 0.05$): if the $p$-value is smaller than $\alpha$, we claim there is evidence to reject the null hypothesis. Hence, small $p$-values indicate that the data support an association between the ordinal response and the $k$th predictor, after adjusting for all other predictors in the model.

Given a significance level $\alpha$, we construct approximate $(1-\alpha)\times 100\%$ confidence intervals for the true $\beta_k$:

$$
\hat{\beta}_k \pm z_{\alpha/2}\,\widehat{\text{se}}\!\left(\hat{\beta}_k\right),
$$

where $z_{\alpha/2}$ is the upper $\alpha/2$ quantile of the Standard Normal distribution. Exponentiating the endpoints of this interval gives a $(1-\alpha)\times 100\%$ confidence interval for the proportional odds ratio $e^{\beta_k}$.

We compute the 95% confidence intervals, odds ratios, and $p$-values below, and filter the significant coefficients at the 5% significance level.

::: {.panel-tabset}

## R Code

```{.r}
library(MASS)

# polr() does not return p-values directly; compute them from the t-values
# using the Normal approximation
coef_table <- coef(summary(ordinal_fit))
p_values   <- 2 * pnorm(abs(coef_table[, "t value"]), lower.tail = FALSE)

# Combine into a tidy inference table for the slope coefficients only
# (thresholds are not included — they are nuisance parameters here)
slope_idx <- rownames(coef_table) %in% names(coef(ordinal_fit))

inference_table <- data.frame(
  term      = rownames(coef_table)[slope_idx],
  estimate  = coef_table[slope_idx, "Value"],
  std_error = coef_table[slope_idx, "Std. Error"],
  statistic = coef_table[slope_idx, "t value"],
  p_value   = p_values[slope_idx]
) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) round(x, 4)))

# Add 95% confidence intervals on the log-odds scale, then exponentiate
ci <- confint(ordinal_fit)   # profile-likelihood CIs (more accurate than Wald)
inference_table$or       <- round(exp(inference_table$estimate), 4)
inference_table$ci_lower <- round(exp(ci[inference_table$term, "2.5 %"]),  4)
inference_table$ci_upper <- round(exp(ci[inference_table$term, "97.5 %"]), 4)

# Print all coefficients
print(inference_table)

# Filter: significant predictors at the 5% level
cat("\nSignificant predictors (p < 0.05):\n")
print(inference_table[inference_table$p_value < 0.05, ])
```

## Python Code

```{.python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from scipy import stats

# Extract estimates, SEs, and compute Wald z-statistics and p-values
params    = ordinal_result.params
std_errs  = ordinal_result.bse
z_stats   = params / std_errs
p_values  = 2 * stats.norm.sf(np.abs(z_stats))

# Confidence intervals on the log-odds scale
ci = ordinal_result.conf_int()

# In statsmodels OrderedModel the threshold parameters contain "/"
# in their names; keep only the slope (predictor) parameters
slope_mask = ~params.index.str.contains("/")

inference_table = pd.DataFrame({
    "term"      : params[slope_mask].index,
    "estimate"  : params[slope_mask].values,
    "std_error" : std_errs[slope_mask].values,
    "statistic" : z_stats[slope_mask].values,
    "p_value"   : p_values[slope_mask],
    "or"        : np.exp(params[slope_mask].values),
    "ci_lower"  : np.exp(ci.loc[slope_mask, "lower"].values),
    "ci_upper"  : np.exp(ci.loc[slope_mask, "upper"].values),
}).round(4)

print("All slope coefficients:")
print(inference_table.to_string(index=False))

print("\nSignificant predictors (p < 0.05):")
print(inference_table[inference_table["p_value"] < 0.05].to_string(index=False))
```

:::

::: {.panel-tabset}

## R Output

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
library(MASS)

coef_table <- coef(summary(ordinal_fit))
p_values   <- 2 * pnorm(abs(coef_table[, "t value"]), lower.tail = FALSE)

slope_idx <- rownames(coef_table) %in% names(coef(ordinal_fit))

inference_table <- data.frame(
  term      = rownames(coef_table)[slope_idx],
  estimate  = coef_table[slope_idx, "Value"],
  std_error = coef_table[slope_idx, "Std. Error"],
  statistic = coef_table[slope_idx, "t value"],
  p_value   = p_values[slope_idx]
) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) round(x, 4)))

ci <- confint(ordinal_fit)
inference_table$or       <- round(exp(inference_table$estimate), 4)
```
